source.title: Next-generation sequencing enables the discovery of more diverse positive clones from a phage-displayed antibody library

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Conventional phage display screening often yields limited diversity of positive clones due to dominance of a few clones after bio-panning. NGS analysis of HCDR3 sequences across panning rounds can reveal enrichment patterns and identify additional diverse binders missed by colony screening.

section_gists.heading: INTRODUCTION
section_gists.gist: Phage display is widely used for antibody discovery but suffers from limited clone diversity after bio-panning. NGS can sequence antibody repertoires, but whole scFv (~750 bp) cannot be sequenced error-free by current platforms. Previous studies used HCDR3 sequencing followed by PCR retrieval. NGS analysis across panning rounds may predict positive clones by enrichment patterns. This study compares clones from conventional colony screening vs. NGS, and classifies NGS clones by enrichment/impoverishment patterns to infer reactivity.
process_signals: Problem: limited diversity from conventional phage display screening
Hypothesis: NGS can reveal more diverse binders and enrichment patterns predict reactivity
Constraint: whole scFv cannot be directly sequenced by NGS; requires HCDR3-based retrieval
methods: NGS of HCDR3 from initial and enriched libraries
Clustering of HCDR3 sequences by enrichment patterns
Two-step linker PCR to retrieve full scFv from HCDR3 sequences
failures_or_limits: NGS cannot sequence entire scFv gene error-free

section_gists.heading: Library construction and bio-panning
section_gists.gist: Three scFv libraries were constructed from PSA-immunized chickens using pComb3XSS phagemid vector. Four rounds of bio-panning were performed using PSA-coated magnetic beads.
process_signals: Experimental design: three independent libraries to assess reproducibility
Standard bio-panning protocol with four rounds
methods: RNA extraction from spleen, bone marrow, bursa
cDNA synthesis and scFv library construction in pComb3XSS

section_gists.heading: Generation of antibody library and screening for positive clones using the conventional colony screening method
section_gists.gist: Three libraries with complexities ~10^10 were constructed. After four panning rounds, 300 clones (100/library) were screened by phage ELISA (OD>0.3). Sanger sequencing identified only 22 unique HCDR3 sequences, demonstrating limited diversity from conventional screening.
process_signals: Baseline problem: only 22 unique HCDR3 sequences from 300 clones across three libraries
Conventional method yields low diversity
failures_or_limits: Limited diversity: only 22 unique binders identified

section_gists.heading: Diversity analysis of antibody clones using next-generation sequencing
section_gists.gist: NGS was performed on phagemid DNA from rounds 0-4 for all three libraries. After quality filtering, 44-53% reads retained. HCDR3 sequences from round 4 were clustered using hierarchical clustering; optimal cluster numbers (2-6) determined by Dunn index. Heatmaps revealed distinct enrichment/impoverishment patterns across rounds.
process_signals: NGS data processing: merging, filtering, alignment to OmpSeq primer
Clustering method: hierarchical clustering with Dunn index to determine optimal clusters
Observation: HCDR3 sequences form clusters with different population dynamics
methods: Preprocessing: merge, filter, align to OmpSeq, trim
HCDR3 extraction and clustering by hierarchical clustering
Dunn index for optimal cluster number
metrics: Optimal clusters: 4 for library 1, 2-3 for libraries 2 and 3

section_gists.heading: Population shift in HCDR3 sequences throughout bio-panning rounds
section_gists.gist: Cluster 1 sequences were abundant initially but impoverished in later rounds. Clusters 2-4 started low and enriched in rounds 3-4. All 22 conventional clones were found in NGS data; 20 belonged to clusters 2-4, 2 to cluster 1. This suggests enrichment patterns correlate with reactivity.
process_signals: Key observation: population shift from cluster 1 to clusters 2-4
Conventional clones mostly in enriched clusters, validating NGS approach
Potential failure: some conventional clones in impoverished cluster 1
methods: Tracking HCDR3 sequence abundance across rounds
Comparison of NGS-identified sequences with conventional clones
metrics: 22 conventional clones all present in NGS data
failures_or_limits: Two conventional positive clones found in impoverished cluster 1 (possible false negatives?)

section_gists.heading: Reactivity of scFv clones identified in NGS analysis
section_gists.gist: 40 new HCDR3 sequences from NGS were selected across clusters. Full scFv genes retrieved by two-step linker PCR and tested by phage ELISA. 85.7% of cluster 1 clones were non-binding (OD<0.2), while 80.8% of clusters 2-4 clones were binding (OD>0.3). Some high-proportion clones (>1%) were missed by conventional screening, possibly due to toxicity or growth interference.
process_signals: Validation: NGS-identified clones from enriched clusters mostly bind; impoverished clusters mostly non-binders
Partial failure: 14.3% of cluster 1 clones bound; 19.2% of clusters 2-4 did not bind
Revision: two-step linker PCR for retrieval; some clones with high NGS proportion not found conventionally, suggesting biological constraints
methods: Arbitrary selection of 1-5 HCDR3 sequences per cluster
Two-step linker PCR to retrieve full scFv
Cloning into phagemid, phage rescue, phage ELISA against PSA
failures_or_limits: Some clones with high NGS proportion not found by conventional screening (possible toxicity)
Not all enriched clones bind; not all impoverished clones are non-binders

section_gists.heading: DISCUSSION
section_gists.gist: NGS provided all conventional HCDR3 sequences plus many new ones. Conventional clones represented only 0.01-6.49% of NGS diversity. 26/40 retrieved NGS clones were positive; some with >1% proportion were missed conventionally, possibly due to toxicity or growth interference. Clones with proportions as low as 0.01% were positive, highlighting NGS's ability to find rare binders. Limitations include inability to sequence full scFv by NGS, cross-priming risk, and need for functional validation.
process_signals: NGS greatly expands diversity discovery
Some high-frequency clones missed by conventional screening due to biological constraints
Rare clones (0.01%) can be positive, impractical to find by colony screening
Limitations: NGS read length, PCR bias, need for functional assays
metrics: Conventional clones: 0.01-6.49% of NGS HCDR3 sequences
Positive clones with proportions as low as 0.01%
failures_or_limits: Some high-proportion clones not retrieved conventionally (toxicity/growth interference)
Binding validation only by phage ELISA; no functional assays

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Conventional phage display screening yields limited diversity due to clonal dominance.
NGS of HCDR3 across panning rounds can identify enrichment patterns that predict binding.
Clustering HCDR3 sequences by population dynamics reveals distinct groups: impoverished (likely non-binders) and enriched (likely binders).
NGS can discover rare positive clones missed by colony screening, but some high-frequency clones may be missed due to biological constraints.
Two-step PCR retrieval from HCDR3 enables full scFv recovery, but cross-priming is a risk.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Why were some high-frequency NGS clones not found by conventional screening? Toxicity or growth interference?
What is the false positive/negative rate of enrichment-based prediction?
How does PCR bias affect retrieval and representation of clones?
Are the NGS-identified clones functional beyond binding (e.g., neutralization)?
Can the approach be generalized to other targets and library types?

events.summary: Phage display enrichment cannot be controlled, resulting in limited clone diversity.
NGS can identify more clones than conventional methods.
Three chicken scFv libraries were constructed and panned on PSA.
NGS of HCDR3 was performed on phagemid DNA from all rounds.
HCDR3 sequences were clustered by enrichment patterns using hierarchical clustering and Dunn index.
Full scFv genes were retrieved from NGS-identified HCDR3 sequences by two-step linker PCR.
Reactivity of retrieved scFv clones was tested by phage ELISA.
NGS cannot sequence the entire scFv gene due to read length limitations.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction, methods, and initial results including library construction, NGS processing, clustering, and retrieval. It establishes the problem, hypothesis, design, and key computational/experimental steps.

events.summary: NGS analysis performed on phagemid DNA from rounds 0-4 of three libraries, yielding 60,000-180,000 VH sequences per sample.
methods_or_tools: merging, filtering, alignment to OmpSeq primer

events.summary: HCDR3 sequences from round 4 were clustered using hierarchical clustering; optimal cluster numbers (2-6) determined by Dunn index.
Heatmaps revealed distinct enrichment/impoverishment patterns: cluster 1 sequences were abundant initially but impoverished later; clusters 2-4 started low and enriched in rounds 3-4.
All 22 conventional clones were found in NGS data; 20 belonged to enriched clusters 2-4, 2 to impoverished cluster 1, validating NGS approach.
methods_or_tools: comparison of NGS and Sanger sequencing

events.summary: 40 new HCDR3 sequences from NGS were selected across clusters; full scFv genes retrieved by two-step linker PCR and tested by phage ELISA.
Some high-proportion clones (>1%) were missed by conventional screening, possibly due to toxicity or growth interference.
NGS cannot sequence entire scFv gene error-free; cross-priming possible during PCR retrieval.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains the core NGS analysis, clustering, validation of enrichment patterns, retrieval and testing of new clones, and discussion of limitations.

events.summary: Enrichment of HCDR3 during bio-panning is a positive predictor of binding reactivity.
Two-step linker PCR and cloning process is difficult to perform in a high-throughput manner.
NGS analysis of VH region including HCDR3 was performed using specific primers.
Two-step linker PCR was designed to retrieve full scFv gene using primers annealing to HCDR3, LFR1, and HFR4.
Binding reactivity of scFv antibodies from each cluster was compared using ANOVA or Mann-Whitney U-test.
NGS analysis of HCDR3 and two-step linker PCR effectively retrieve antigen-specific scFv clones missed by conventional screening.
Enrichment of HCDR3 sequence over bio-panning is a positive predictive factor for binding reactivity.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains validation of enrichment as predictor, limitation of PCR retrieval, design of NGS and two-step PCR, and statistical analysis of binding reactivity.

evidence_bank.quote: a major drawback of phage display technology is that because the degree of the enrichment cannot be controlled during the bio-panning process, it frequently results in a limited number of clones
we applied next-generation sequencing (NGS) to screen clones from a library and determine whether a greater number of clones can be identified using NGS than using conventional methods
Three white leghorn chickens were immunized and boosted four times with recombinant human PSA
three phage-displayed libraries of chicken scFvs were constructed using the pComb3XSS phagemid vector
Four rounds of bio-panning were performed to screen scFv clones from the library
A total of 15 sets of phagemid DNA including three initial chicken scFv libraries and three libraries obtained after each of four rounds of bio-panning were analyzed using a MiSeq system
The sequencing data was processed by CLC Genomics Workbench version 5
The cleaned-up sequencing data were processed by merging the paired-end sequence reads using fast length adjustment of short reads
Sequencing data were further cleaned up using PRINSEQ
a custom Perl script was used to summarize and count sequence reads
An optimized number of clusters in the merged sequence read counts was estimated using the clValid algorithm
the assessment for each and every clustering is represented in the Dunn index
Unsupervised hierarchical clustering analysis was used to cluster HCDR3 sequences according to the number of clusters estimated by clValid
Ward's method was used to measure distances between sequence reads based on read counts throughout the bio-panning
To rebuild real scFv clones from the virtual HCDR3 sequences in the clusters, we performed two-step linker PCR
The second PCR step linked these two gene fragments into a single scFv gene
The scFv gene was ligated into the pComb3XSS phagemid vector and rescued as scFv-displaying phages
To measure the binding reactivity of these scFv-displaying phages, we rescued more than 15 clones per HCDR3 sequence, and performed phage enzyme immunoassay
We regarded the clone providing the highest optical density at 405 nm as the retrieved clone
sequencing of the entire single-chain variable fragment (scFv) gene, which contains 750–800 bases, could not be achieved using any of these NGS platforms
A total of 15 sets of phagemid DNA (three chicken libraries from bio-panning rounds 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4) were used for NGS analysis.
44–53% of the original sequences were retained after aligning with OmpSeq primer sequence
clValid predicted that 2–6 clusters would be the most dependable in the HCDR3 sequence count profile data (Table 3).
The sequence reads in chicken library 1 showed the maximum Dunn index (0.1048) with 4–6 clusters, and chicken libraries 2 and 3 had maximum Dunn indices with 2–3 clusters.
HCDR3 sequences in cluster 1 were highly abundant before bio-panning and up to the second bio-panning round. However, there was a sudden impoverishment in rounds 3 and 4 of bio-panning.

In contrast, HCDR3 sequences that belonged to clusters 2 and 3 (including cluster 4 of library 1) showed the opposite pattern. Their populations were very low before bio-panning, remained low after the second round of bio-panning, and started to enrich from the third round of bio-panning.

All 22 HCDR3 sequences in clones found via the conventional colony screening method existed among the HCDR3 sequences obtained from NGS analysis of phagemid DNA prepared after the fourth round of bio-panning (Table 1).
Two out of the 22 unique HCDR3 sequences belonged to cluster 1, and the other 20 HCDR3 sequences belonged to clusters 2, 3 or 4.
For each cluster, 1–5 HCDR3 sequences newly identified from the fourth round of bio-panning via NGS analysis were selected arbitrarily (Table 4).
These selected sequences were used to synthesize the primers to retrieve the whole scFv gene from the phagemid DNA.
The scFv gene was prepared in two-step linker PCR using the primers and cloned into a phagemid vector (Figure 3).

Why these clones with such high proportions were not identified in the conventional colony screening method is not clear; however, it might be caused by either inherent toxicity with phage assembly or interference from bacterial growth.
Using currently available NGS tools, it is not possible to sequence the entire scFv gene, which is about 750 bp in length, without error.
However, there is always the possibility of cross-priming between clones during the PCR process.
when a certain HCDR3 sequence was enriched through the bio-panning process, the clone with the HCDR3 is more likely to be positive; in this study over 80% (21 out of 26 clones)
this two-step linker PCR and cloning process is very difficult to perform in a high-throughput manner
For NGS analysis, most of VH region including HCDR3 was amplified and sequenced using specific primers
To retrieve scFv gene, two-step linker PCR was performed using primers annealing to HCDR3, LFR1 and HFR4.
ANOVA with Turkey's multiple-comparison test was used to compare cluster 1 with other clusters. In library 3, the P-value was calculated using the Mann–Whitney U-test.

NGS analysis of the HCDR3 sequence, and two-step linker PCR using PCR primers based on this sequence, provide an effective way to retrieve antigen-specific scFv clones that are difficult to identify by the conventional colony screening method.
Enrichment of the HCDR3 sequence over the bio-panning process is a positive predictive factor in the selection of scFv clones harboring binding reactivity.

The proportion of two positive clones in library 1 and library 3 were 2.
Four clones in library 1 and one clone in library 3 exhibited a proportion of $>1\%$ among the HCDR3 sequences obtained after the fourth round.

These results imply that antibody clones with low reactivity tend to be impoverished throughout bio-panning (cluster 1), in contrast to the antibody clones with high reactivity, which showed enrichment throughout bio-panning (clusters 2\~4).
In contrast, HCDR3 sequences that belonged to clusters 2 and 3 (including cluster 4 of library 1) showed the opposite pattern.
HCDR3 sequences in cluster 1 were highly abundant before bio-panning and up to the second bio-panning round.

We clustered HCDR3 sequences into 2–6 clusters using hierarchical clustering, and generated heat maps for each cluster to examine the patterns of HCDR3 sequence enrichment and population shift throughout the bio-panning rounds.

In the first PCR step, primers targeting both LFR1-HCDR3 (LFR1\_F primer, 5'-GTGG CCCAGGCGGCCCTG-3') and HCDR3-HFR4 fragments (HFR4\_R primer, 5'-CTGGCCGGCCTGGCCACT-3') were synthesized, based on HCDR3 sequences determined in NGS analysis and phagemid DNA obtained after the 4th round of bio-panning.
Table 1 HCDR3 amino-acid sequences selected using the conventional colony screening method, and binding reactivity measurement of the antibody clones
We also classified scFv clones obtained from NGS into 3–4 clusters based on their enrichment or impoverishment patterns, and analyzed these patterns for clues regarding the reactivity of scFv clones.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Conventional phage display screening yields limited diversity of positive clones due to uncontrolled enrichment. NGS analysis of HCDR3 sequences across panning rounds can reveal enrichment patterns and identify additional diverse binders missed by colony screening.

canonical_events.summary: Phage display enrichment cannot be controlled, resulting in limited clone diversity.
NGS can identify more clones than conventional methods.
Three chicken scFv libraries were constructed and panned on PSA.
Conventional colony screening of 300 clones yielded only 22 unique HCDR3 sequences.
NGS of HCDR3 was performed on phagemid DNA from all rounds, yielding 60,000-180,000 VH sequences per sample.
HCDR3 sequences were clustered by enrichment patterns using hierarchical clustering and Dunn index.
Heatmaps revealed distinct enrichment/impoverishment patterns: cluster 1 impoverished, clusters 2-4 enriched.
All 22 conventional clones were found in NGS data; 20 belonged to enriched clusters 2-4, 2 to impoverished cluster 1.
40 new HCDR3 sequences from NGS were selected across clusters; full scFv genes retrieved by two-step linker PCR and tested by phage ELISA.
85.7% of cluster 1 clones were non-binding, while 80.8% of clusters 2-4 clones were binding.
Some high-proportion clones (>1%) were missed by conventional screening, possibly due to toxicity or growth interference.
NGS cannot sequence entire scFv gene error-free; cross-priming possible during PCR retrieval.
Two-step linker PCR and cloning process is difficult to perform in a high-throughput manner.
NGS analysis of HCDR3 and two-step linker PCR effectively retrieve antigen-specific scFv clones missed by conventional screening.

edges.reason: The problem of limited diversity motivates the hypothesis that NGS can identify more clones.
The hypothesis leads to the design of constructing libraries and panning on PSA to test NGS.
After library construction and panning, conventional screening is performed to establish baseline diversity.
Conventional screening results motivate NGS analysis to explore deeper diversity.
NGS data enables clustering of HCDR3 sequences by enrichment patterns.
Clustering results are visualized as heatmaps to reveal enrichment/impoverishment patterns.
The enrichment patterns are validated by checking that conventional clones mostly fall into enriched clusters.
Validation of clustering approach enables selection of new HCDR3 sequences for retrieval and testing.
Retrieval and ELISA testing of new clones provides binding reactivity data.
High binding reactivity of some NGS clones contrasts with their absence in conventional screening, indicating a failure of conventional methods.
The retrieval process highlights limitations of NGS read length and PCR cross-priming.
The two-step linker PCR method is noted as a limitation for high-throughput application.
The overall effectiveness of NGS and retrieval is validated by the successful identification of new binders.

must_cover_points: Problem of limited diversity in conventional phage display
Hypothesis that NGS can identify more clones
Conventional screening yields only 22 unique HCDR3 sequences
NGS of HCDR3 across panning rounds
Clustering of HCDR3 sequences by enrichment patterns
Validation that conventional clones mostly in enriched clusters
Retrieval of new scFv clones by two-step linker PCR
Binding reactivity results: 85.7% non-binding in cluster 1, 80.8% binding in clusters 2-4
Failure: high-proportion clones missed by conventional screening
Limitation: NGS cannot sequence full scFv; cross-priming risk
Overall validation that NGS + retrieval effectively finds missed binders
